subsections.title: Lambda & Functional Interfaces
subsections.keyRule: Lambda = concise implementation of a functional interface (interface with exactly ONE abstract method). @FunctionalInterface enforces this. Key built-ins: Function<T,R>, Predicate<T>, Consumer<T>, Supplier<T>, BiFunction<T,U,R>. Lambdas replaced anonymous inner classes.

terms.what: Lambda is a short, anonymous function. Instead of creating an object to hold one method, you write the logic inline with arrow syntax: (params) -> body.
terms.why: Before Java 8, passing behavior required anonymous inner classes — 5+ lines for a simple comparator. Lambdas express the same in one line.
terms.how: (a, b) -> a.compareTo(b) is a Comparator. x -> x > 0 is a Predicate. () -> 'hello' is a Supplier. str -> System.out.println(str) is a Consumer.
terms.where: Anywhere a functional interface is expected: Collections.sort(), Stream API, CompletableFuture, @Async, event listeners.
terms.when: Use lambdas for short, one-method behaviors. If logic is more than 3-4 lines, extract to a named method for readability.
terms.issue: Lambda capturing a mutable local variable fails — variables captured by lambda must be effectively final.
terms.fix: Make captured variables final or effectively final. If you need to mutate, use an AtomicInteger or an instance field.

star.s: Sort employee list by salary — 5-line anonymous class.

terms.what: Function<T,R> takes one input of type T, returns one output of type R. It is the most versatile built-in functional interface.
terms.why: Represents transformations — convert input to output. Map one type to another.
terms.how: Function<String,Integer> strLen = s -> s.length(); strLen.apply('hello') → 5. Chain: func1.andThen(func2) — output of func1 is input of func2.
terms.where: Stream.map(), transformation pipelines, data conversion, DTO mapping.
terms.when: Use Function when transforming data from one type to another.
terms.issue: null input causes NPE in the lambda body if not handled.

star.s: DTO to Entity mapping function needed.
star.a: Function<UserDTO, User> toEntity = dto -> new User(dto.getName(), dto.getEmail()).

terms.what: Predicate<T> takes input of type T, returns boolean. Represents a test/filter condition.
terms.why: Extracts filter logic from loops into reusable, composable conditions.
terms.how: Predicate<Employee> highSalary = e -> e.getSalary() > 100000; stream.filter(highSalary). Combine: pred1.and(pred2), pred1.or(pred2), pred1.negate().
terms.where: Stream.filter(), input validation, access control checks, business rule testing.
terms.when: Use Predicate for any condition that returns true/false. Compose complex conditions with and()/or()/negate().
terms.issue: Complex Predicate chains can become hard to read.
terms.fix: Name each Predicate clearly: Predicate<User> isActive = ...; Predicate<User> isAdmin = ...; isActive.and(isAdmin) reads naturally.

star.s: User search with multiple optional filters.
star.a: Built Predicate for each filter. Combined with and(). stream.filter(combined).
star.r: Each filter independently testable. Easy to add new filters.

terms.what: Consumer<T> takes one input, returns nothing (void). Represents a side-effect operation — do something with T but produce no output.
terms.why: For operations that consume data: logging, persisting, printing, sending notifications.
terms.how: Consumer<String> logger = msg -> System.out.println('[LOG] ' + msg); list.forEach(logger). Chain: consumer1.andThen(consumer2).
terms.where: Stream.forEach(), Iterable.forEach(), event handlers, side-effect operations.
terms.when: Use Consumer for side-effects: logging, persisting, displaying. Not for transformations (use Function for those).
terms.issue: Multiple side effects in one Consumer lambda — hard to test each effect separately.
terms.fix: Separate into individual Consumers and chain with andThen(): logConsumer.andThen(saveConsumer).andThen(notifyConsumer).

star.s: Processing pipeline needs to log, save, and notify for each item.
star.a: Three separate Consumers chained with andThen(). Each independently testable.

terms.what: Supplier<T> takes no input, produces a value of type T. Represents lazy value creation — 'give me a value when asked'.
terms.why: For lazy initialization, default value factories, and providing values on demand without computing them upfront.
terms.where: Optional.orElseGet(), lazy initialization, test fixtures, configuration providers.
terms.when: Use Supplier when computation should be deferred — not done until the value is actually needed.
terms.issue: orElse() evaluates the default eagerly even if the Optional has a value. orElseGet() is lazy — uses Supplier.
terms.fix: Prefer orElseGet(supplier) over orElse(default) when the default involves any computation or object creation.

star.s: Optional.orElse(expensiveComputation()) called even when optional has value.

terms.what: Method reference is a shorthand for a lambda that calls one existing method. Uses :: notation. Four types: static, instance, instance of arbitrary type, constructor.
terms.why: More readable than lambdas for simple method calls. Self-documenting.
terms.how: Static: Integer::parseInt. Instance of specific object: myList::add. Instance of arbitrary type: String::length (applied to each String). Constructor: User::new.
terms.where: Stream operations, Comparator.comparing(), forEach, any place a lambda calls exactly one method.
terms.when: Use method reference when lambda body is just one method call. Use lambda when there is any additional logic.

subsections.title: Lambda Internals & Closures
subsections.keyRule: Lambda captures variables from enclosing scope — they must be effectively final (not reassigned). Lambda 'this' refers to the enclosing class, not the lambda itself. Type inference lets compiler determine functional interface type from context.

terms.what: A lambda can capture local variables from its enclosing method, but those variables must be effectively final — assigned once and never changed again.
terms.why: Lambdas can run later in a different thread. If the variable changed after capture, the lambda would have stale or unexpected data.
terms.how: int x = 10; Runnable r = () -> System.out.println(x); // OK — x never changed. int y = 10; y = 20; Runnable r2 = () -> System.out.println(y); // COMPILE ERROR — y is reassigned.
terms.where: Any lambda or anonymous class capturing local variables.
terms.when: When you need to capture a value that changes in a loop: copy it to a final local variable before the lambda.
terms.issue: Loop variable capture: for(int i=0; i<10; i++) { threads.add(() -> print(i)); } — compile error. i changes.
terms.fix: final int captured = i; threads.add(() -> print(captured)); — each iteration captures its own copy.

star.s: Lambda in loop captures wrong values.
star.a: For each iteration, assign loop variable to a new effectively-final local. Each lambda captures its own copy.

terms.what: In a lambda, 'this' refers to the enclosing class instance. In an anonymous class, 'this' refers to the anonymous class instance itself.
terms.why: Lambda is not a class — it has no 'this'. Anonymous class IS a class — it has its own 'this'.
terms.how: class Outer { Runnable lambda = () -> System.out.println(this); // 'this' = Outer. Runnable anon = new Runnable() { public void run() { System.out.println(this); } }; // 'this' = anonymous Runnable }
terms.where: Event handlers, callbacks, Spring @EventListener — important distinction when accessing enclosing class fields.
terms.when: Use lambda when you want to access the enclosing class's fields via 'this'. Use anonymous class only when you need the inner class's own identity.
terms.issue: Using 'this' in anonymous class when you meant to access outer class — wrong reference, NPE or wrong behavior.
terms.fix: Prefer lambdas over anonymous classes. Lambda 'this' = enclosing class — simpler and clearer.

star.s: Anonymous class callback: 'this.service' throws NPE — 'this' refers to anonymous class which has no service field.
star.a: Changed to lambda. 'this.service' now refers to outer class correctly.

terms.what: Java compiler infers the lambda's type from context — you don't need to specify the functional interface type if it's unambiguous.
terms.why: Reduces boilerplate. Comparator<Employee> c = (a, b) -> a.getName().compareTo(b.getName()); — no need to write Employee types in the lambda.
terms.how: List<String> list = Arrays.asList('b','a','c'); list.sort((a, b) -> a.compareTo(b)); — compiler infers a and b are String from List<String> context.
terms.where: Lambda parameters, var in local variable type inference, method reference type inference.
terms.when: Let inference work. Only add explicit types when: ambiguity exists, or the types make the code clearer.
terms.issue: Ambiguous context confuses compiler: list.stream().collect(toUnmodifiableList()) — sometimes needs explicit type.
terms.fix: Add explicit type parameter: .<String>toUnmodifiableList() or just add cast to resolve ambiguity.

star.a: Method overload creates ambiguity. Added explicit parameter type in lambda to resolve.

subsections.title: Additional Functional Interfaces
subsections.keyRule: BiFunction<T,U,R>: two inputs, one output. BiPredicate<T,U>: two inputs, boolean output. BiConsumer<T,U>: two inputs, no output. UnaryOperator<T>: one input, same type output. BinaryOperator<T>: two same-type inputs, same type output. IntFunction/ToIntFunction for primitive-to-object/object-to-int.

terms.what: BiConsumer<T,U> takes two parameters and returns nothing (void). For two-input operations with side effects.
terms.why: Regular Consumer handles one parameter. Many side-effect operations naturally need two inputs — print key+value, log field+message.
terms.how: BiConsumer<String,Integer> print = (k,v) -> System.out.println(k + '=' + v); map.forEach(print); — Map.forEach takes a BiConsumer.
terms.where: Map.forEach(), Map.replaceAll(), BiFunction with side effects.
terms.when: Use BiConsumer for Map iteration, logging two values, writing key-value pairs.
terms.issue: BiConsumer is void — cannot chain results. For chaining, use BiFunction.
terms.fix: BiConsumer supports andThen(): biConsumer.andThen(biConsumer2) executes both in sequence.

star.s: Stream.reduce needs a combining function of two values → same type.

terms.what: UnaryOperator<T> extends Function<T,T> — same type in and out. BinaryOperator<T> extends BiFunction<T,T,T> — two same-type inputs, same type output.
terms.why: Specialized for operations where input and output type are the same — transform in place, combine two values of same type.
terms.how: UnaryOperator<String> upper = String::toUpperCase; list.replaceAll(upper); — transforms each element. BinaryOperator<Integer> sum = Integer::sum; stream.reduce(0, sum);
terms.where: List.replaceAll(), Stream.reduce(), math operations on same type, string transformations.
terms.when: Use UnaryOperator instead of Function<T,T> for clarity. BinaryOperator instead of BiFunction<T,T,T>.
terms.issue: UnaryOperator is a subtype of Function — can pass to any method expecting Function<T,T>.
terms.fix: No fix needed — use them for semantic clarity. UnaryOperator makes the 'same type in/out' contract explicit.

star.s: Need to uppercase all strings in a list in-place.

terms.what: IntFunction<R> takes an int and returns R. ToIntFunction<T> takes T and returns int. Primitive-specialized to avoid boxing overhead.
terms.why: Boxing int to Integer and back has cost. Primitive functional interfaces avoid it for numeric operations.
terms.how: IntFunction<String> intToStr = Integer::toString; // int → String. ToIntFunction<String> len = String::length; // String → int. int[] lengths = strings.stream().mapToInt(len).toArray();
terms.where: Stream.mapToInt(), IntStream operations, performance-critical numeric processing.
terms.when: Use primitive functional interfaces when working with int/long/double streams to avoid boxing.
terms.issue: Using Function<Integer,Integer> instead of IntUnaryOperator — every call boxes and unboxes.
terms.fix: Use specialized primitive interfaces: IntUnaryOperator, LongFunction, DoubleConsumer, etc. for better performance.

star.s: Stream of 10M numbers using Function<Integer,Integer> — slow due to boxing.
star.r: No boxing. 3x faster for numeric pipeline.
